title: 第 43 節
en: That this is the right view of the case is confirmed by the remarks made by Vigwana-Bhikshu in his Yoga-s&rasamgraha, p. 18. Here we are told that even when there is some imperfection in the employment of the above means (faith, energy, memory, absorbing meditation, and knowledge), the two results (absorption and liberation) can be brought very near by the grace of the Parama-lsvara, the Highest Lord, and secured by devotion to Him-.

By Parama-lsvara or the Highest Lord is here meant that particular Purusha (Self) who was never touched by the five troubles, nescience and the rest, nor by virtue or TRUE OBJECT OF YOGA. 335 vice and their various developments, or by any residue (results of former deeds) in general. Vigwana-Bhikshu abstains from saying much more on the Lord, because, as he says, he has treated of this Being very fully in his remarks on the Brahma-Sutras I, i. He probably refers to his commentary on the Vedanta; and he is evidently quite convinced that, however different the roads followed 'by the Vedantins and Samkhya-yogins may be, the Divine idea of both schools is much the same. He only adds that the powers and omniscience of the Isvara are equalled or excelled by none, that he is the spiritual chief and father of all the gods, such as Brahma, Vislmu, and Hara, that he imparts spiritual vision ((?;lana-/jakshus) through the Vedas, and that he is the inner guide, and called rra^iava. Devotion to Him is said to consist in contemplation and to end in direct perception. Steadfastness with regard to Isvara is represented as the principal factor in abstract meditation and in liberation, because it leads to greater nearness to the final goal, steadiness with regard to the human self being secondary only. This devotion to Isvara is also declared to put an end to all the impediments, such as illness, &c. (I, 30) ; and a passage is quoted from the Smriti, ' For one desiring liberation the most comfortable path is clinging to or resting on Vistmu ; otherwise, thinking only with the mind, a man is sure to be deceived.'
True Object of Yoga.

It is clear throughout the whole of this chapter on Isvara that devotion to him is no more than one of the means, though, it may be, a very important one, for the attainment of liberation, the highest goal of the Yoga. But it is not that highest goal itself, but only a means towards it, nor could it be accepted as the most important feature of the Yoga. The really important character of the Yoga consists in its teaching that, however true the Samkhya-philosophy may be, it fails to accomplish its end without those practical helps which the Yoga-philosophy alone supplies, The human mind, though fully enlightened as, to its true nature, would soon be carried away again by the torrent 336 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

of life ; the impressions of the senses and all the cares and troubles of every-day life would return, if there were no means of making the mind as firm as a rock. Now this steadying of the mind, this Yoga, is what Pata/?</ali is chiefly concerned with.
-ffitta.

We saw that in the second Sutra he explained Yoga as ffitta-vritti-nirodha, that is, restraining or steadying the actions and distractions of thought. Vritti, which I translate by action, has also been rendered by movement or function ; while JTitta, which I give as thought, has often been translated by mind or the thinking principle. It is curious that the Yoga should have employed a word which, as far as I know, was not a recognised technical term of the Samkhya. In the S&mkhya, the term would be Manas, mind, but Manas in a state of activity, and, of course, as a development of Ahamkara and Buddhi. It has to be taken here as a psychological term, as a name for thought, as carried on in real life, and indirectly only of the instrument of thought. As I had to use mind for Manas in the Samkhya-philosophy, it would be difficult to find a better rendering of the word when used by Yoga philosophers. Of course Manas is always different from Buddhi, in so far as it is a modification of Buddhi, which itself has passed through Ahamkara or the differentiation of subjectivity and objectivity. But for practical purposes, what 'is meant by .ffitta is simply our ^nought or our thinking, and though mind, with us also, has been defined very differently by different philosophers, and is used most promiscuously in common parlance, its etymological relationship with Manas pointed it out as the most convenient rendering of Manas, provided always that we remember its being a technical term of the Yoga-philosophy, as we have to do whenever we render Praknti by nature. Nirodha, restraint, does not mean entire suppression of all movements of thought, but at first concentration only, though it leads in the end to something like utter vacuity or self -absorption. In all the functions of the Manas it must be remembered that the real self-conscious seer or perceiver is, for the time FUNCTIONS OF THE MIND. 337 being, the Purusha or Self. It is he who is temporarily interested in what is going on, though not absorbed in it except by a delusion only. Like the moon reflected in the ripples of the waters, the Self appears as moving in the waves which break against it from the vast ocean of Prakriti, but in reality it is not moving. We saw that the mind, when receiving impressions from the outer world, was supposed in Hindu philosophy to assume for the time being the actual form of the object perceived, but, when once perfect in Yoga, it perceives nothing but itself.
Functions of the Mind.
The principal acts and functions of the mind are described as right notion, wrong notion, f^ncy, sleep, and remembering, and they may be either painful or not.

Eight notions are brought about by the three PramS/nag, "so well known from different systems of Indian philosophy, as sensuous perception, inference, and testimony, Vedic or otherwise. It is significant ^that Pata//#ali should have used^Agama instead of the Aptava/cana of the S&mkhya, for Agama means distinctly the Veda, and thus would establish once for all what is called the orthodox character of the Toga.

Wrong notions require no explanation. They are illustrated by our mistaking mother-of-pearl for silver, a rope for a snake, &c. A state of doubt also when we are uncertain whether what we see at a distance is a man or the trunk of a tree, is classed among wrong notions.

Fancy is explained as chiefly due to words ; and a curious instance of fancy is given when we speak of the intelligence of the Self or Purusha, or of the head of R&hu, the fact being that there is no intelligence belonging to Self, but that the Self is altogether intelligence, just as R&hu, the monster that is supposed to swallow the moon, is not, a being that has a head, but is a head and nothing else.
Sleep is defined as that state (Vritti) of the mind which has nothing for its object. The com'mentator, however, explains that in sleep also a kind of perception must take 33*5 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
place, because, otherwise, we could not say that we had slept well or badly.

Remembering is the not wiping out of an object that has once been perceived. While true perception, false perception, and fancy take place in a waking state, a dream, which is a perception of vivid impressions, takes place in sleep, while sleep itself has no perceptible object. Remembering may depend on true or false perceptions, on fancy, and even on dreams.
Exercises.
Now all these actions or functions have to be restrained, and in the end to be suppressed, and this is said to be effected by exercises (Abhy^sa) and freedom from passions (Vairagya), I, 12.

Indian philosophers have the excellent habit of always explaining the meaning of their technical terms. Having introduced for the first time the terms exercise and freedom from passion, Pata/7#ali asks at once: 'What is Abhy&sa or exercise ? ' Abhyasa is generally used in the sense of repetition, but he answers that he means hereafter to use this term in the sense of effort towards steadiness (Sthiti) of thought. And if it be asked what is meant by steadiness or Sthiti, he declares that it means that state of the mind, when, free from all activity (Vritti), it remains in its own character, that is, unchanged. Such effort must be continuous or repeated, as implied by the term Abhyasa (1, 13).
This Abhy&sa is said to become firmly grounded, if practised for a long time thoroughly and unintermittingly Dispassion, Vair&gya.

Next follows the definition of dispassion (VaMgya), as the consciousness of having overcome (the world) on the part of one who has no longer any desire for any objects whatsoever, whether visible or revealed (I, 15).

Here visible^(Dr£shtfa) stands for perceptible or sensuous objects, while Anusr&vika may be translated by revealed, as it^is derived from Anusrava, and this is identical with Sicuii or Veda. Perhaps Anusrava is more general than DISPASSION., VAIBAGYA. 339 Veda, including all that has been handed down, such as the stories about the happiness of the gods in paradise (Devaloka), &c. The consciousness of* having subdued or overcome all such desires and being no longer the slave o£ them, that, we are told, is Vairagya or dispassionateness, and that is the highest point which the student of Yogaphilosophy hopes to reach.

It is interesting to see how deeply this idea of Vairagya or dispassionateness must have entered into the daily life of the Hindus. Ii> is constantly mentioned as the highest excellence not for ascetics only, but for everybody. It sometimes does not mean much more than what we mean by the even and subdued temper of the true gentleman, but it signifies also the highest unworldliness and a complete surrender of all selfish desires. A very good description of what Vairagya is or ought to be is preserved to us in the hundred verses ascribed to Bhartrihari (650 A. p.), which are preceded by two other centuries of verses, one on worldly wisdom and the other on love. Many of these verses occur again and again in other works, and it is very doubtful whether Bhartrihari was really the original author of them all, or whether he only collected them aa Subhashitas 1. Anyhow they show how the philosophy of Vairagya had leavened the popular mind of India at that distant time, nor has it ceased to do so to the present day. It was perhaps bold, after Bhartrihari, to undertake a similar collection of verses on the same subject. But as the Vair&gya-sataka of CrainaMrya seems in more recent times to have acquired considerable popularity in India, a few extracts from it may serve to show that the old teaching of Patatf^ali and Bhartrihari has not yet been forgotten in their native country.

c Death follows man like a shadow, and pursues him like an enemy ; perform, therefore, good deeds, so that you may reap a blessing hereafter/ ' Frequent enjoyment of earthly prosperity has led to your sufferings. Pity it is that you have not tried the " Know Yourself. "'
1 His work is actually called Subhashita-trisati, see Eeport of Sanskrit and Tamil MSS., 1896-97, by Seshagiri Sastri, p. 7.
place, because, otherwise, we could not say that we had slept well or badly.

Remembering is the not wiping out of an object that has once been perceived. While true perception, false perception, and fancy take place in a waking state, a dream, which is a perception of vivid impressions, takes place in sleep, while sleep itself has no perceptible object. Remembering may depend on true or false perceptions, on fancy, and even on dreams.
Exercises.
Now all these actions or functions have to be restrained, and in the end to be suppressed, and this is said to be effected by exercises (Abhyasa) and freedom from passions (Vair&gya), I, 12.

Indian philosophers have the excellent habit of always explaining the meaning of their technical terms. Having introduced for the first time the terms exercise and freedom from passion, Pata;?$ali asks at once : * What is Abhyasa or exercise 1 ' Abhyasa is generally used in the sense of repetition, but he answers that he means hereafter to use this term in the sense of effort towards steadiness (Sthiti) of thought. And if it be asked what is meant by steadiness or Sthiti, he declares that it means that state of the mind, when, free from all activity (Vntti), it remains in its own character, that is, unchanged. Such effort must be continuous or repeated, as implied by the term Abhy&sa (1, 13).
This Abhyasa is said to become firmly grounded, if practised for a long time thoroughly and unintermittingly ft *4> Dispassion, Vair&gya.
zh: 維格瓦那-比克舒（Vigwana-Bhikshu）在其《瑜伽精髓集》（Yoga-sarasamgraha）第18頁的論述證實了這正是對此案例的正確理解。在該處我們被告知，即使在上述手段（信心、精進、憶念、專注禪定與智慧）的運用中存有某些不完善之處，這兩種結果（三摩地與解脫）仍可藉由最高主宰帕拉瑪-伊瓦拉（Parama-Isvara）的恩典而被帶得極為接近，並可藉由對祂的虔信而得以保障。

此處「至高之主」（Parama-lsvara）係指那特定的自我（Purusha），其從未為五種困擾（不知與其他困擾）、或德行與罪惡及其各種衍生物、或任何一般的業報殘留（前生行為之結果）所觸及。毗濕瓦那‧比克舒（Vigwana-Bhikshu）對於談論至主甚少著墨，蓋如其所言，彼已在其對《梵經》第一卷第一章的註疏中詳盡論述此存有。彼或即指其對《韋丹塔》（Vedanta）的註解；且彼顯然深信，儘管韋丹塔派與數論瑜伽派所循之道路迥異，兩派之神聖觀念實大同小異。彼僅補充謂伊濕瓦拉（Isvara）之威能與全知性無人能比或超越，彼為一切提婆（諸如梵天、毗濕奴與哈拉等）之精神領袖與父親，彼透過吠陀賜予精神視覺（知識眼光‧jnana-jakshus），且彼為內在引導者，被稱為救度者（ tra^iava）。對於祂之虔敬據云包含於冥思，終至於直接知覺。對於伊濕瓦拉之堅定，被表現為抽象冥想與解脫中之主要因素，蓋其引向更加接近終極目標，唯對於人之自我之堅定乃為其次。此對於伊濕瓦拉之虔敬亦被宣稱可終止一切障礙，如疾病等（第一卷第三十節）；且引述《法經》之一段文句：「欲求解脫者，最舒適之道乃依靠或安住於毗濕奴；否則，人若唯以心思，必然遭欺矣。」
瑜伽的真實目的

在這整章關於伊斯瓦拉（Isvara）的論述中，清楚可見對他的虔敬不過是達成解脫——瑜伽的最高目標——的諸多手段之一，儘管它可能是一個非常重要的手段。但它並非那個最高目標本身，而只是趨向於它的一種手段，也不能被接受為瑜伽的最重要特徵。瑜伽真正重要的性質在於其教義表明，儘管數論哲學（Samkhya-philosophy）可能是真理，但若沒有瑜伽哲學單獨提供的那些實踐上的助力，它就無法達成其目的。人類心靈，儘管對其真正本性已充分啟蒙，也將很快被洪流再次沖走
的生命；感官的印象以及日常生活中的種種煩憂與困頓都會重新襲來，若無手段使心志如磐石般堅定。現在，這種心志的穩固，這瑜伽（Yoga），正是帕坦加利（Patañjali）主要關切之事。
我已準備好進行翻譯，但發現您提供的英文原文只有「-ffitta.」這個片段，這似乎不是完整的內容。 請提供完整的英文原文段落，我將按照您列出的規則，為您翻譯成繁體中文。

我們看到在第二經文（Sutra）中，他將瑜伽解釋為「心識作用的制止」（ffitta-vritti-nirodha），即抑制或穩定思想的作用與散亂。Vritti 一詞，我譯為「作用」，也曾被譯為「運動」或「機能」；而 ffitta 一詞，我譯為「思想」，則經常被譯為「心」或「思考原理」。好奇的是，瑜伽派所採用的字詞，據我所知，並非數論派（Samkhya）公認的技術術語。在數論派中，相應的術語應為「心」（Manas），但此心乃處於活動狀態的心，當然是作為「我執」（Ahamkara）與「智慧」（Buddhi）的發展而存在。這裡應將其視為心理學術語，作為實際生活中所進行之思想的名稱，且僅以間接方式指涉思想的工具。既然我在數論哲學中必須用「心」來譯「Manas」，當瑜伽哲學家使用該詞時，便很難找到更恰當的譯詞。當然，在「Manas」乃「心」的修正形式此一意義上，Manas 總是不同於「智慧」（Buddhi），而「智慧」本身則已透過「我執」（Ahamkara）——即主觀性與客觀性的分化——而經歷演化。但就實際目的而言，ffitta 所指的不過是我們的「思想」或「思考」；儘管「心」在我們這裡也已被不同哲學家定義得極為懸殊，且在日常用語中被使用得最為混雜，其詞源與 Manas 的關聯性使其成為最便利的譯法，前提當然是我們必須謹記它乃瑜伽哲學的技術術語，就如同我們在用「自然」來譯 Prakriti 時所必須做的一樣。「制止」（Nirodha）並非指思想所有運動的完全壓制，而首先僅指專注，儘管最終它會導致某種絕對的空無或自我融合。在「心」（Manas）的所有機能中，必須銘記的是，真正自我意識的見證者或感知者在當下乃是「自我」（Purusha）。正是他暫時關注著所發生的一切，儘管他並不為此所迷執，除非透過妄想。如同月亮倒映在水波紋中，「自我」似乎在從廣大的自然界（Prakriti）大洋衝擊而來的波浪中運動，然而實際上它根本不在運動。我們看到心念在接收外在世界的印象時，據印度教哲學所說，應暫時假現所感知對象的實際形式，但一旦瑜伽達至完美，它便唯有覺知於自身。
心靈的功能
心靈的主要作用與功能被描述為正念、邪念、想像、睡眠與記憶，而這些作用與功能可能帶來痛苦，亦可能不然。
八種概念是由三種量（Pramā/na）所產生的，這三種量在印度哲學的各個系統中都眾所周知，即感官知覺、推理與證言（無論是吠陀的或其他的證言）。值得注意的是，帕坦佳利（Patañjali）應該使用了阿迦摩（Āgama）而非數論派（Sāṁkhya）的正智者言說（Āptavācana），因為阿迦摩明確地意指吠陀，因此將一勞永逸地確立所謂的瑜伽的正統性格。
錯誤的觀念無須解釋。它們可由我們將珠母誤認為銀、將繩子誤認為蛇等例子來說明。懷疑的狀態也被歸類於錯誤的觀念之中，即當我們對於遠處所見之物究竟是人還是樹幹感到不確定時。
想像力主要被解釋為源於語言；當我們談及自我（Self）或普魯沙（Purusha）的智慧，或羅睺（Rāhu）的頭顱時，便給出了一個奇特的想像力例證。事實上，自我並不具有智慧，而是自我本身就是整體的智慧；正如羅睺這個據說吞噬月亮的怪物一樣，並非具有頭顱的生命體，而是頭顱本身，別無他物。
睡眠被定義為心靈的那種狀態（Vritti），它沒有任何對象。然而，註釋者解釋說，在睡眠中也必然存在某種知覺。
地位，因為否則我們無法說我們睡得好或睡得不好。
記憶是對曾經被感知之對象的不抹除。真正的感知、虛假的感知與想像皆發生於清醒狀態，而夢——一種對生動印象的感知——發生於睡眠之中，睡眠本身則無可感知的對象。記憶可依賴於真正的或虛假的感知、想像，甚至夢。
練習題
現在所有這些行為或功能都必須被制約，最終要被克制，據說這是透過練習（Abhyāsa）和無欲（Vairagya）而達成的，卷一，第12頁。

印度哲學家有一個極好的習慣，即總是詳細解釋其技術術語的含義。帕坦伽利（Patañjali）在首次引入練習（exercise）和離欲（freedom from passion）這兩個概念後，立刻提出詢問：「何謂阿卜亥薩（Abhyasa）或練習？」阿卜亥薩通常被用以表示重複之意，但他說明自己此後欲將此術語用於指向思想之穩定性（Sthiti）的努力。若問及穩定性或斯緹蒂（Sthiti）之義，他宣稱其意乃心之一種狀態，即心離於一切活動（Vritti），而安住於其本性，亦即保持不變。如此的努力必須是持續的或重複的，此乃阿卜亥薩（Abhyasa）一詞所內含之義（1, 13）。
據說這種習練（Abhyāsa）若長期徹底且不間斷地修習，就會變得根深蒂固。出離心（Vairāgya）。
接著是對無執著（Vairāgya）的定義，即一個人不再對任何事物有任何欲望（無論是可見的或啟示的），因而克服（世界）的意識（I, 15）。

這裡，可見的（Drshtfa）代表可感知的或感官的對象，而 Anusrvika 可譯為「啟示的」，因為它源自 Anusrava，而這與 Shruti 或吠陀相同。也許 Anusrava 比吠陀更為廣泛，包括所有代代相傳的內容，例如關於諸神在天國（Devaloka）中幸福的故事等等。克制或克服了所有此類欲望、不再成為它們的奴隸的意識，我們被告知，這便是無執著（Vairagya）或無欲，這是瑜伽哲學的修行者所期望達到的最高境界。

看到無執著（Vairagya）這一觀念如何深入印度人的日常生活，實在令人感到興味盎然。它被不斷提及為最高的卓越品德，不僅對於苦行者，而且對於每一個人都是如此。有時它的含義不過是我們所謂真正紳士那種平和而沈靜的氣質，但它也表示最高的出世精神，以及對一切自我私欲的完全捨棄。關於無執著是什麼或應該是什麼的一份十分良好的描述，被保存在歸屬於跋多羅瑜訶（Bhartrihari，西元650年左右）的百首詩頌中，這些詩頌之前還有另外兩個百首詩集，一部涉及俗世智慧，另一部涉及愛情。這些詩頌中的許多在其他著作中反覆出現，因此非常值得懷疑的是，跋多羅瑜訶是否真的是它們全部的原始作者，抑或他只是將它們作為妙言集（Subhashitas）編纂而已。無論如何，它們表明無執著的哲學在那個遙遠的時代是如何滲透到印度人的大眾思想中的，而且到了今日它仍未停止這樣做。在跋多羅瑜訶之後，承擔編纂類似的同一主題的詩頌集合也許是大膽的。但由於《無執著百頌》（Vairagya-sataka）這部作品在較近的時期似乎在印度獲得了相當的流行，從中摘取的若干片段也許可以用來說明，帕坦伽利（Patañjali）和跋多羅瑜訶的古老教導在它們的祖國還未被遺忘。
死亡如影子般跟隨著人類，如敵人般追逐著人類；因此，行善積德，如此你方能在來世獲得祝福。『頻繁享受塵世繁榮導致了你的苦難。遺憾的是你未曾試過「認識你自己」。』
他的著作實際上名為《三百詩頌》（Subhashita-trisati），見 Seshagiri Sastri 著《梵文及泰米爾手稿報告》（Report of Sanskrit and Tamil MSS.），1896-97 年，第 7 頁。
之處，因為，否則，我們就無法說我們睡得好或睡得不好。
記憶是對曾經被感知的對象不加消除，使其持續存在。真正的知覺、虛假的知覺與想像發生在清醒狀態中，而夢——一種對生動印象的知覺——發生在睡眠中，睡眠本身則沒有可感知的對象。記憶可能取決於真正的知覺或虛假的知覺、想像，甚至夢。
習題。
現在，所有這些行為或機能都必須加以制約，最終必須被壓制，據說這是通過修習（Abhyasa）和離欲（Vairāgya）而達成的，I, 12。

印度哲學家有一項優良的習慣，即總是解釋其技術術語的含義。帕坦迦利（Patañjali）在首次引入「修習」（exercise）和「離欲」（freedom from passion）這兩個術語後，隨即提出：「何謂 Abhyāsa 或修習？」Abhyāsa 通常用於重複的意義，但他解釋說，他以後將用此術語指涉思想趨向穩定性（Sthiti）的努力。若有人詢問何謂穩定性或 Sthiti，他則宣稱它意指心靈的一種狀態，當心靈免除一切活動（Vṛtti）時，它便保持在其自身的性質之中，亦即保持不變。這種努力必須是連續的或重複的，如同 Abhyāsa 一詞所暗示的意思（1, 13）。
據說這種修習（Abhyasa）若長期徹底地不間斷地實踐，就能牢固地確立自身。《4》離欲（Vairāgya）。
